173
    Thus “information professionals” are subject in French law to an obligation of “objectivity,” which is
part of the right to one’s image. See Lamberterie and Strubel, L’Image Manipulée, 357-358; [L. Marino,
Responsabilité civile, activité d’iniformation et médias (1997)]
174
    Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée, 111-122; Manfred Weiss and Barbara Geck, Worker Privacy in
Germany, 17 Comparative Labor Law Journal 75 (1995). These issues are explored more extensively in
Whitman and Friedman, European Transformation of Harassment Law.
175
    Martine Bourrie-Quenillet and Florence Rodhain, L’Utilisation de la messagerie electronique dans
l’entreprise. Aspects juridiques et managériaux en France et aux Etats-Unis, La Semaine Juridique no. 2,
January 9, 2002

57
 “personal sphere.”176 It goes well beyond emails and the like, too, to cover a wide of

issues touching questions of workplace dignity. One striking French decision, for

example, found it a violation of dignity rights when the employer in a retail store required

that employees show a receipt for merchandise that they wished to take home. Treating

workers with that sort of suspiciousness was regarded by the court as a violation of their

expectation to be treated as honorable persons. 177

The contrast with American approaches to the workplace is telling. American

privacy protections, at their metaphoric core, are the sorts of protections afforded by the

walls of one’s home. They have been extended beyond the literal home, of course, since

the eighteenth century. Nevertheless, it remains the case that American protections

become progressively weaker the further the affected person is from home. This is

particularly true when courts apply the “reasonable expectation of privacy” test

developed in the Fourth Amendment context. 178 The primary locus of one’s “reasonable

expectation of privacy” is of course in the home, and persons outside the home have

correspondingly few privacy protections. This applies to workers as well, whose

expectation of privacy in the workplace, according to the American cases, is sometimes

close to nil. 179

The same contrast holds in one of the most striking aspects of the comparative

law of privacy: the treatment of criminal offenders, accused persons, and prison inmates.

Continental privacy protections extend to these classes of persons as well. Continental

privacy law has been strongly concerned with the privacy rights of persons caught up in

expectation of privacy” test).
179
    E.g. Thompson v. Johnson County Community College, 930 F. Supp. 501 (D. Kan. 1996).
